nativeRange.habitat: Found over sand and coral bottoms of clear channels and reef flats at shallow depths, typically from the surface down to around 6 m. This species is possibly endemic to Kapingamarangi Atoll in the Caroline Islands.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on benthic algae in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer marine-based seaweed (nori) on a clip at least three times per week, supplemented with spirulina-based pellets/flakes and occasional meaty foods such as mysis or brine shrimp. A vegetable-heavy diet is commonly considered important for supporting immune function, coloration, and reduced aggression.

summary: The Mimic Half Black Tang (Acanthurus chronixis) is a rarely encountered surgeonfish from the Western Pacific, possibly endemic to Kapingamarangi Atoll in the Caroline Islands. It is generally considered reef-safe and tends to be aggressive toward other tangs and similarly shaped fish.
careNotes:
The Mimic Half Black Tang (Acanthurus chronixis) is a rarely-encountered surgeonfish that, in the wild, is possibly endemic to Kapingamarangi Atoll in the Caroline Islands (Western Pacific). Adults have been described as dark brown with a darker band at the base of the dorsal fin and a distinctive contrasting half-black caudal/posterior region; juveniles are reported to display lighter tan coloration with black trim.

Like all Acanthurus surgeonfish, this species carries a sharp, blade-like caudal-peduncle spine (scalpel) that can inflict deep cuts on handlers and tankmates — caution is generally advised when netting or transferring. Tangs are also commonly considered prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when kept in poor water quality or fed an algae-poor diet, so a varied vegetable-heavy diet and stable water quality are commonly considered important.

This species has been known to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other surgeonfish, but tends to be peaceful with unrelated fish. It is generally advised to keep only one specimen per aquarium unless the system is very large. A well-established reef tank with ample live rock for grazing, strong water movement, and open swimming space is generally preferred.

Captive longevity is poorly documented for this species; estimates of 5–10 years are considered plausible based on what is known about similar Acanthurus surgeonfish.
